enriched_metadata.objective: Predict survival of Titanic passengers based on demographic, socioeconomic, and travel information from the infamous 1912 maritime disaster
enriched_metadata.sources: Historical Titanic passenger records - Original competition dataset from Kaggle Titanic competition
enriched_metadata.features_description: Passenger demographic and travel information: class, name, sex, age, siblings/spouses aboard, parents/children aboard, ticket number, fare, cabin, port of embarkation
enriched_metadata.representativity_description: Training set with 891 passengers from the Titanic's maiden voyage; represents a subset of the 2,224 people aboard with focus on passengers with complete survival records
enriched_metadata.sample_balance_description: Imbalanced dataset with approximately 38% survivors (342) and 62% fatalities (549), reflecting the tragic reality of insufficient lifeboats
enriched_metadata.missing_values_description: Missing values primarily in Age (~20% missing) and Cabin (~77% missing) columns due to incomplete historical record keeping

historical_significance.significance: One of the most famous maritime disasters in history, widely studied for social class disparities in survival rates
historical_significance.cultural_impact: The 'Women and children first' protocol and class-based survival differences make this a compelling case study
historical_significance.educational_legacy: Most popular beginner dataset in machine learning for binary classification problems

classes.description: Third class - Lower class passengers and immigrants

feature_details.name: Survived
feature_details.description: Target variable - survival outcome (0 = No, 1 = Yes)

feature_details.name: Pclass
feature_details.description: Ticket class as proxy for socioeconomic status (1 = 1st, 2 = 2nd, 3 = 3rd)

feature_details.name: Name
feature_details.description: Passenger name (contains titles that can be extracted)

feature_details.name: Age
feature_details.description: Age in years (missing for ~20% of passengers)

feature_details.name: SibSp
feature_details.description: Number of siblings/spouses aboard the Titanic

feature_details.name: Parch
feature_details.description: Number of parents/children aboard the Titanic

ml_applications.typical_results: Accuracy around 80-85% achievable with proper feature engineering

common_exercises: Explore survival rates by passenger class and gender
Handle missing Age and Cabin data
Build and compare multiple classification models
Analyze feature importance and model interpretability

ethical_considerations: Historical dataset reflecting social inequalities of early 20th century
Class-based survival disparities raise questions about social justice
Gender and age biases in survival protocols
Use of human tragedy data requires respectful treatment

historical_context.social_structure: Rigid class hierarchy reflected in ship accommodation
historical_context.maritime_law: Insufficient lifeboat regulation leading to disaster
historical_context.cultural_norms: Women and children first protocol, class-based privileges

lessons_learned: Importance of adequate safety measures regardless of class
Maritime safety regulations were revolutionized post-Titanic
Social inequality can be literally life-or-death in crisis situations

research_questions: What factors most influenced survival probability?
How did social class affect life-or-death outcomes?
Can we identify optimal evacuation strategies?
What does survival data tell us about Edwardian society?
statistical_insights: Clear correlation between passenger class and survival
Women and children had significantly higher survival rates
Family groups showed complex survival patterns
Fare paid strongly correlated with class and survival

enriched_metadata.data_errors_description: Historical records have natural inconsistencies; missing values in age and cabin assignments due to incomplete record keeping
